serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of fresh parsley is approximately 1 tablespoon of chopped leaves (about 4 g), used as a garnish or flavouring in cooked dishes, salads, and sauces.

nutrient_source.title: Parsley, fresh — Singapore HPB Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11297)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fresh parsley (FDC ID 170416). Values are consistent between both sources. Saturated fat, sugar, and additional micronutrients supplemented from USDA SR Legacy where HPB did not report them.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of herbs and low-carbohydrate vegetables — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database reference values for non-starchy leafy herbs
gi_source.source_match_notes: Parsley has no direct GI entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. Given its very low carbohydrate content (6.33 g/100g, of which 3.3 g is dietary fibre, yielding approximately 3 g available carbohydrate per 100g), and its classification as a non-starchy leafy herb, a GI of approximately 5 is estimated by analogy with other low-carbohydrate herbs and leafy greens documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per typical 4 g serving is negligible (<0.1) and rounded to 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (56 mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (554 mg/100g) with a potassium-to-sodium ratio of approximately 10:1
hypertension.guidance: Parsley is strongly DASH-diet aligned; its high potassium and low sodium content support vascular tone and blood pressure reduction, and it can be used freely as a culinary herb in hypertensive patients without restriction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low available carbohydrate (~3 g/100g net), high dietary fibre (3.3 g/100g), negligible sugar (0.85 g/100g), and flavonoid myricetin with evidence for improving insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Parsley has a negligible glycaemic impact at culinary serving sizes and can be used freely by people with Type 2 Diabetes; its myricetin content may provide additive insulin-sensitising benefit when consumed consistently as part of a varied diet.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, very low saturated fat (0.13 g/100g), high potassium, high folate (152 mcg/100g), and high vitamin C (133 mg/100g) with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory flavonoids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Parsley contributes negligible saturated fat and cholesterol while providing folate, potassium, and antioxidant flavonoids (apigenin, luteolin) that support endothelial function and reduce homocysteine-related cardiovascular risk; it is appropriate for unrestricted use in cardiovascular disease management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (133 mg/100g), vitamin K, and anti-inflammatory flavonoids apigenin and luteolin
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Parsley's high vitamin C and flavonoid content may reduce oxidative stress and inflammatory markers relevant to rheumatoid arthritis, but patients on warfarin should maintain consistent parsley intake due to its high vitamin K content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Parsley (Petroselinum crispum) is a bright green biennial herb native to the Mediterranean region, widely cultivated and used globally as a culinary herb, garnish, and flavouring ingredient. Two main varieties are used in cooking: flat-leaf (Italian) parsley, which has a stronger flavour, and curly-leaf parsley, which is milder and more commonly used as a garnish. Fresh parsley has a clean, slightly peppery, grassy flavour with mild bitterness. It is used raw as a garnish, chopped into salads, stirred into sauces such as chimichurri and gremolata, and added to soups, stews, and grain dishes. Nutritionally, parsley is a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate herb (36 kcal/100g) with a notable micronutrient profile: it is exceptionally rich in vitamin K, vitamin C (133 mg/100g), vitamin A (421 mcg RAE/100g), folate (152 mcg/100g), iron (6.2 mg/100g), calcium (138 mg/100g), and potassium (554 mg/100g). Its sodium content is low (56 mg/100g), making it strongly DASH-diet compatible. Parsley contains bioactive flavonoids including apigenin, luteolin, and myricetin, which have demonstrated antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and insulin-sensitising properties in research settings. Clinically, parsley is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Its high vitamin K content is relevant for patients on anticoagulant therapy (warfarin), who should maintain consistent rather than variable intake. Parsley is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is used in cuisines worldwide including Middle Eastern, Mediterranean, European, and Southeast Asian cooking.
common_user_queries: Is parsley good for high blood pressure?
What is the difference between flat-leaf and curly parsley?
Is parsley safe for people on warfarin?
How much potassium is in parsley?
Can I eat parsley every day?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source) with confidence 0.95, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170416). Micronutrient values are consistent between both approved sources. GI is an analogous estimate for a non-starchy herb with negligible available carbohydrate, which is a well-supported estimation approach.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg/100g) for a plant-based herb — naturally zero as parsley contains no animal-derived components. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mg/100g and vitamin D is 0.0 mcg/100g — both naturally zero for a plant-based food with no animal or fortified components. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100g — naturally zero for an unprocessed fresh herb. Saturated fat (0.13 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.29 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (0.12 g/100g), sugar (0.85 g/100g), and additional micronutrients (vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, folate, minerals) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170416 where HPB did not report these fields. GI estimated by analogy with non-starchy leafy herbs from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI entry exists for parsley. Glycemic load per 4 g serving is negligible (<0.1), rounded to 0.0. High vitamin K content (not quantified in HPB source; approximately 1640 mcg/100g per USDA) is clinically relevant for patients on warfarin anticoagulation therapy. Parsley is used in small culinary quantities (typically 2–10 g per serving); all health benefits are additive at culinary doses. Large medicinal doses of parsley are not recommended during pregnancy or for patients with kidney disease.
